text: Hello, this video is part of our patient education series looking at robotic repair of a large parasophageal hernia with a toupee fundoplication.
The surgeon places standard eight-millimeter robotic trocars, including a liver retractor, in the subxiphoid space for the hernia repair procedure.
The stomach is visibly protruding through the crura, with the liver and diaphragm clearly in view.
The surgeon reduces the herniated stomach from above the diaphragmatic hiatus back into the abdominal cavity, revealing the large portion of the stomach that had protruded into the chest.
The video shows chronic irritation at the diaphragmatic crura due to the hernia, with visible thickening of the tissue, particularly on the anterior portion.
The surgeon opens the pars flaccida to expose the right crus of the diaphragm, revealing the anatomical structures involved in the hernia repair.
An orogastric tube is passed into the stomach, which is visibly distended with air, and the stomach is deflated to facilitate the surgical procedure.
The surgeon opens the peritoneum overlying the right crura to access the hernia sac, with the surrounding anatomical structures such as the liver and diaphragm visible in the surgical field.
A portion of the peritoneum overlying the cruce is left intact for closure, revealing the underlying muscle structure.

The surgeon dissects over the anterior portion of the diaphragmatic hiatus, freeing the hernia sac from surrounding structures, including the visible lung pleura, and continues the dissection towards the left side.
The surgeon performs a posterior dissection to further expose the left cruz of the diaphragm, creating a visible window in the tissue.
The surgeon opens the greater curvature of the stomach and extends the incision up to the left crux of the diaphragm, revealing the anatomical structures involved in the hernia repair.
The hernia sac is visible on the anterior portion of the gastroesophageal junction, with a clear view of the right crux, left crux, and the esophagus passing through the diaphragmatic hiatus.
The surgeon dissects the hernia sac from the anterior portion of the stomach, moving towards the esophagus, preparing to remove it from the abdominal cavity.
The anterior vagus nerve is visualized and carefully preserved, ensuring it remains free of injury. The posterior vagus nerve is also preserved without injury during the procedure.
The surgeon removes the hernia sac in its entirety from the anterior portion of the gastroesophageal junction and extracts it from the abdominal cavity.
A Penrose drain is looped around the gastroesophageal junction and secured with an endo loop, providing retraction for the dissection of the esophagus within the mediastinum.
The surgeon performs a 360-degree dissection around the esophagus within the mediastinum, aiming to achieve sufficient laxity of the gastroesophageal junction below the diaphragmatic hiatus.
The video shows extensive chronic scarring within the mediastinum, visibly causing retraction of the esophagus and stomach into the mediastinum.
The surgeon dissects posterior to the esophagus, revealing critical structures such as the aorta, the right and left pleura, and the esophagus, which is visible at the top of the screen.

The gastroesophageal junction is visibly positioned more than three centimeters below the diaphragmatic hiatus, indicating sufficient laxity achieved without downward tension. Sutures are introduced into the surgical field.
The surgeon begins the hiatal closure using a unidirectional barbed permanent suture, specifically a Prolene Stratafix suture, to secure the diaphragmatic hiatus.

The surgeon uses large bites of the crura for hiatal closure, sizing the closure to accommodate a large grasping forcep. This technique aims to minimize recurrence risk while ensuring sufficient laxity to prevent swallowing difficulties.

Mesh is generally not utilized as the long-term data has not demonstrated any significant decrease in recurrences at five years using a biologic absorbable mesh and synthetic mesh is not utilized in these situations as there would be risk of esophageal erosion.
The surgeon initiates the fundoplication by bringing the fundus posterior to the esophagus. A shoeshine maneuver is performed to confirm sufficient laxity of the fundus around the esophagus.
The surgeon places three sutures from the stomach to the hiatal closure, initiating the Toupee fundoplication technique.
The surgeon performs a 270-degree partial wrap using a slip knot technique to apply sufficient tension on the sutures during the closure.

I generally perform a two hundred and seventy degree toupee partial fundoplication as opposed to the three hundred and sixty degree missing complete fundoplication. As the data has shown equivalent incidents of recurrence with equivalent reflux control and a lower rate of undesirable side effects such as dysphagia and gas bloat.
The surgeon places permanent sutures from the esophagus to the stomach at the ten o'clock position, carefully avoiding the anterior vagus nerves. A slip knot is used to secure the sutures.

The surgeon places three to four sutures from the stomach to the esophagus at the two o'clock position. A forced suture is placed from the stomach to the upper portion of the stomach at the gastroesophageal junction. The final suture is placed from the left crura to the stomach posteriorly.

The surgeon cuts and removes the Penrose drain, followed by the visualization of the completed wrap. The needles and Penrose drain are extracted from the abdominal cavity, and the liver retractor is withdrawn, marking the completion of the procedure.
